evals.prompt: I just got a Framework Desktop with the AMD Ryzen AI Max+ 395 and 128GB of RAM running Ubuntu 24.04. I want to run Llama 3.1 70B on it with llama.cpp. How do I build it and what settings should I use for the best token generation speed? I've already installed ROCm 6.3.
evals.expected_output: Should provide: 1) TTM memory fix as the critical first step, 2) Build command with GPU_TARGETS=gfx1150 and GGML_HIP=ON, 3) ROCBLAS_USE_HIPBLASLT=1 environment variable, 4) Runtime flags including -ngl 99, --no-mmap, -fa on, -ctk q8_0 -ctv q8_0, 5) Recommendation for Q4_K_M quantization to fit in memory

assertions.name: ttm_memory_fix
assertions.description: Mentions TTM pages_limit configuration for Linux

assertions.name: correct_gpu_target
assertions.description: Specifies GPU_TARGETS=gfx1150 (or gfx1151) in the build command

assertions.name: hipblaslt_env
assertions.description: Includes ROCBLAS_USE_HIPBLASLT=1 environment variable

assertions.name: no_mmap_flag
assertions.description: Recommends --no-mmap for the 70B model

assertions.name: full_gpu_offload
assertions.description: Uses -ngl 99 to offload all layers to GPU

assertions.name: quantization_recommendation
assertions.description: Recommends an appropriate quantization (Q4_K_M or similar) for fitting 70B in 128GB

evals.prompt: I'm getting terrible prompt processing speed on my Strix Halo laptop - only about 350 tokens/sec on a 7B model. Token generation is fine at ~48 t/s. What's wrong?
evals.expected_output: Should identify the rocBLAS kernel issue for gfx1150/gfx1151 and recommend ROCBLAS_USE_HIPBLASLT=1, explaining that this can improve pp speed from ~350 to ~880 t/s. Should also check if GPU_TARGETS was set correctly during build.

assertions.name: identifies_rocblas_issue
assertions.description: Identifies that the problem is poorly optimized rocBLAS kernels for gfx1150/gfx1151

assertions.name: hipblaslt_solution
assertions.description: Recommends setting ROCBLAS_USE_HIPBLASLT=1

assertions.name: expected_improvement
assertions.description: Mentions the expected ~2.5x improvement (from ~350 to ~880 t/s)

evals.prompt: My llama.cpp keeps running out of memory when loading a 65B model on my Ryzen AI Max 395 even though I have 128GB RAM. rocm-smi only shows 4GB VRAM. What's going on?
evals.expected_output: Should explain: 1) Strix Halo uses unified memory, the 4GB is just the BIOS-configured 'dedicated' portion, 2) The TTM pages_limit needs to be increased to allow GPU to access more system memory, 3) Provide the exact modprobe.d configuration, 4) After TTM fix, recommend --no-mmap and -ngl 99

assertions.name: explains_unified_memory
assertions.description: Explains that Strix Halo uses unified memory and the 4GB is just the dedicated VRAM portion

assertions.name: ttm_fix
assertions.description: Provides TTM pages_limit fix with the modprobe.d configuration

assertions.name: reboot_required
assertions.description: Mentions that a reboot or initramfs update is needed after TTM change

evals.prompt: I have a Strix Halo system but I don't want to install ROCm - it's too heavy. Can I just use Vulkan for llama.cpp? I already have mesa drivers. How does Vulkan compare to HIP on this hardware?
evals.expected_output: Should explain: 1) Yes, Vulkan works — build with GGML_VULKAN=ON, no GPU_TARGETS needed since Vulkan uses SPIR-V shaders, 2) Vulkan auto-detects RDNA 3.5 and uses wave64 subgroups, 3) HIP is recommended over Vulkan for best performance due to hipBLASLt and HIP graphs, 4) Vulkan has known model loading issues (#18741), 5) Vulkan is a valid fallback when ROCm isn't practical

assertions.name: vulkan_build_command
assertions.description: Provides Vulkan build command with GGML_VULKAN=ON

assertions.name: hip_recommended_over_vulkan
assertions.description: Recommends HIP/ROCm over Vulkan for best performance on Strix Halo

assertions.name: vulkan_known_issues
assertions.description: Mentions Vulkan model loading issues or known limitations on Strix Halo

assertions.name: vulkan_as_valid_fallback
assertions.description: Acknowledges Vulkan as a reasonable alternative when ROCm is impractical

evals.prompt: I want to run a small 7B BF16 model on my Ryzen AI Max 395 using only the CPU — no GPU at all. I heard Zen 5 has good AVX-512 and there's something called ZenDNN. How do I set this up for best CPU-only performance?
evals.expected_output: Should explain: 1) Build with GGML_ZENDNN=ON and GGML_NATIVE=ON for Zen 5 feature auto-detection, 2) Set ZENDNNL_MATMUL_ALGO=1 for best performance, 3) BF16 is the ideal format for ZenDNN on Zen 5 (uses _mm512_dpbf16_ps), 4) Use -ngl 0 and -t 16 (physical cores), 5) ZenDNN only accelerates FP32/BF16 matmul — quantized models use standard AVX-512 GGML kernels

assertions.name: zendnn_build_flags
assertions.description: Provides build command with GGML_ZENDNN=ON

assertions.name: zendnn_matmul_algo
assertions.description: Sets ZENDNNL_MATMUL_ALGO=1 environment variable

assertions.name: bf16_recommendation
assertions.description: Recommends BF16 format as ideal for ZenDNN on Zen 5

assertions.name: thread_count
assertions.description: Recommends using physical core count (16 cores) for thread count

evals.prompt: I need to deploy llama-server on my Strix Halo workstation as a production service for our small team (4-5 concurrent users). We're using a Llama 3 8B Q4_K_M model. What's the optimal configuration for stability and throughput?
evals.expected_output: Should provide: 1) Full llama-server command with -np 4 or 5, -c for context length, -ngl 99, --no-mmap, -fa on, -ctk q8_0, 2) ROCBLAS_USE_HIPBLASLT=1, 3) KV cache quantization since parallel sequences each need their own cache, 4) Low thread count (-t 4) for GPU-primary, 5) Monitoring advice (rocm-smi, /health endpoint), 6) Consider systemd for persistence

assertions.name: parallel_sequences
assertions.description: Configures -np for multiple concurrent users (4 or 5)

assertions.name: kv_cache_quantization
assertions.description: Recommends KV cache quantization (-ctk q8_0 or similar) since parallel sequences multiply memory usage

assertions.name: server_runtime_flags
assertions.description: Includes essential flags: -ngl 99, --no-mmap, ROCBLAS_USE_HIPBLASLT=1

assertions.name: production_stability
assertions.description: Mentions monitoring, systemd service, or health endpoint for production stability
